clean_story: Breaking without shattering.  Strength from cracks. "Failure resilience" describes how systems—or people—absorb shocks, bend, and return stronger, like a tree swaying in a storm instead of snapping. Imagine dropping a ceramic mug versus a rubber ball. The mug cracks—the ball bounces. Resilience isn’t about avoiding the fall. It’s about designing the bounce. Bridges use "ductile iron" that stretches under stress, preventing sudden collapses. Antifragile bones rebuild thicker after micro-fractures from running. Even Tokyo’s skyscrapers sway 3 meters in earthquakes—flexible joints turn destruction into motion. The trick?Small, controlled failures act as warnings. Aircraft engines shed weak turbine blades during tests—so the final design never does mid-flight. Startups "fail fast" to pivot before resources drain. Nature’s version?Forest fires clear deadwood, letting sunlight reach new shoots. Without burns, ecosystems stagnate. Three related subjects are adaptive systems, stress inoculation, and antifragility.
